title: Finite-Sample Selected Covariance Spectra in Classical Shadows
abstract: We study finite-sample estimation of selected covariance matrices of classical-shadow outputs. For a general shadow-output vector, we consider its covariance matrix and a fixed selected compression. Our main theorem applies to arbitrary shadow protocols and gives an operator-norm error bound for the selected sample-centered empirical covariance. When the protocol-dependent constants appearing in this bound remain independent of the ambient system size, the required sample size is also independent of the ambient dimension. The proof combines matrix Bernstein concentration, an exact rank-one centering identity, and Weyl and Davis--Kahan perturbation bounds.   We verify this bounded-output condition for local measurement settings. For general local product shadow protocols with fixed local dimension, finite-weight product observables lead to bounds controlled by support sizes and local reconstruction coefficients, not by the total number of tensor factors. Hence uniform bounds on selected set size, observable weight, and local reconstruction coefficients imply dimension-independent selected covariance estimation.   For biased local Pauli shadows, we evaluate the relevant bound in closed form from the selected Pauli supports and local basis-selection probabilities. We also derive an exact covariance formula governed by Pauli compatibility and inverse-probability overlap factors, showing how measurement bias affects both diagonal variances and off-diagonal statistical couplings. A comparison with global Clifford shadows shows that this dimension-independent local behavior is not automatic for every shadow protocol.
summary: This paper develops a rigorous finite-sample theory for estimating the covariance spectrum of quantum measurements reconstructed via classical shadows. It proves that for local measurement settings, the required sample size to estimate the selected covariance is independent of the total system dimension. This is a significant result for quantum tomography, as it shows that local structure can overcome the curse of dimensionality.
main_problem: The paper addresses the finite-sample estimation of selected covariance matrices derived from classical-shadow outputs in quantum measurements.
main_result: It establishes dimension-independent sample size bounds for selected covariance estimation when the measurement protocol is local (e.g., local Pauli shadows), contrasting this with protocols where the required sample size scales with system dimension.
method: The analysis combines matrix Bernstein concentration inequalities with exact rank-one centering identities to control the error in the sample-centered empirical covariance matrix.
model_or_system: The study uses the framework of classical shadows, where quantum state tomography is approximated by measuring a set of observables (Pauli strings) and reconstructing a shadow snapshot. The system is analyzed in terms of local product measurements versus global measurements.
key_observables: Selected covariance matrix ($\Sigma_l(ho)$), shadow-output vector ($\hat{x}$), and the selected one-shot radius ($B_l$).
important_parameters: The selected dimension ($l$), the local support sizes, and the local reconstruction coefficients are critical parameters determining the sample complexity.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption for the dimension-independent result is that the selected set of observables (or their associated coefficients) is bounded and local in nature (e.g., local product shadows).
figures_summary: The notes mention TABLE I comparing the work to other literature strands and TABLE II/III defining notation for local/global measurements, but no specific figure content is detailed.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by first defining the shadow-output vector and covariance, then proving the general finite-sample theorem, specializing to local product shadows, and finally deriving explicit, dimension-independent covariance formulas for biased local Pauli shadows.
why_interesting: It provides rigorous, quantitative tools for characterizing the statistical information contained in quantum measurements (shadows), which is crucial for understanding the limits of quantum state reconstruction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
